articles.title: In First School Visit, President Urges Atlanta Crowd to Support ‘Trump Accounts’
articles.summary: MARIETTA, Ga. — In his first public school visit since taking office last year, President Donald Trump stopped at an Atlanta-area high school Wednesday to promote his administration’s effort to kickstart savings accounts for the nation’s children. For decades, he said, Washington politicians gave young people “nothing but debt,” he said. “The government is finally […]
articles.full_body:
Title: In First School Visit, President Urges Atlanta Crowd to Support ‘Trump Accounts’

URL Source: https://www.the74million.org/article/in-first-school-visit-president-urges-atlanta-crowd-to-support-trump-accounts

Published Time: 2026-07-22T19:15:07-04:00

Markdown Content:
_Get stories like this delivered straight to your inbox._[**Sign up for The 74 Newsletter**](https://www.the74million.org/about/newsletters/?utm_source=website&utm_medium=article&utm_campaign=top&utm_id=newsletter)

MARIETTA, Ga. — In his first public school visit since taking office last year, President Donald Trump stopped at an Atlanta-area high school Wednesday to promote his administration’s effort to kickstart savings accounts for the nation’s children.

For decades, he said, Washington politicians gave young people “nothing but debt,” he said.

“The government is finally giving money back to our children,” he told the crowd at Wheeler High School in Cobb County, inviting families who have opened accounts f to join him on stage with their children.

The event brought together several cabinet members who joined the president in urging families to open Trump Accounts. Before Trump took the stage in the school’s large gymnasium, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, Treasurer Brandon Beach, Education Secretary Linda McMahon and Kelly Loeffler, a former Georgia senator who now serves as head of the Small Business Administration, all described the child savings policy as transformational.

McMahon called it “one of the largest generational investments in upward mobility that our country has ever made.”

Under the savings initiative, which went live July 4, the administration will give $1,000 deposits to children born during the president’s term if their parents open a Trump Account. Over seven million children are [already enrolled](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/06/23/trump-account-signups-hit-6-million-millions-more-children-eligible.html) in the program.

The child savings account program was included in last year’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act.

Parents and others can contribute [up to $5,000 per year](https://trumpaccounts.gov/) to the tax-deferred account until the child turns 18 and converts it into a traditional individual retirement account.

Philanthropists Michael and Susan Dell expanded on the government’s contribution last year when they [pitched in $6.25 billion](https://apnews.com/article/michael-dell-susan-trump-accounts-stock-market-poverty-inequality-7e2615d50a3fc0563109ed0eeb4c41e1) to provide an extra $250 for 25 million children 10 and under living in ZIP codes with median incomes below $150,000. Other donors have since made contributions, some for specific states.

Chris Woods, a financial adviser and Cobb district parent, told The 74 that he thinks the policy is a wise move.

“There are a lot of ways to save for children’s college and their future,” he said. “The difference is you’re getting funds from the government.”

But Eli Speer, a recent high school graduate and Trump supporter, said the program isn’t “fiscally conservative.”

“We have to prioritize shaving down the debt, cutting red tape,” he said.

![Image 1](https://www.the74million.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/trump-wheeler-high-school.png)

Recent high school graduates were among those who gathered in the early morning hours Wednesday to attend President Donald Trump’s visit at Wheeler High School (Linda Jacobson/The 74)

The [libertarian Cato Institute](https://www.cato.org/policy-analysis/improving-trump-accounts#overview-other-qualified-accounts) agreed, calling Trump Accounts a “government welfare program” that further complicates the tax system and might not be a better investment opportunity than existing options. Others question whether the families who would benefit the most from the program are even aware of it. An [Urban Institute analysis](https://www.urban.org/urban-wire/awareness-trump-accounts-lowest-among-families-who-stand-gain-most) showed that higher-earning adults and those who already have at least $5,000 in savings were more likely to know about the program.

Bessent, however, pushed back on criticisms that the policy will favor wealthier families. Of the families who have already opened accounts for their kids, he said 86% earn household incomes of $200,000 or less.

“Trump Accounts are reaching the very people they were designed to serve,” he said. “President Trump is creating assets for the American people, not more debt and broken promises.”

Despite the strong turnout Wednesday, Cobb County is not actually “Trump country,” as several speakers called it. Trump lost Cobb all three times he ran for president. The district, however, is led by a majority Republican board.

“The school board is the last bastion of Republican control that Cobb County has,” said Heather Tolley-Bauer, a co-founder of Watching the Funds-Cobb, a watchdog group that [raised questions](https://www.facebook.com/photo/?fbid=1651587876974230&set=pcb.1651590226973995) this week about whether district resources were being used to support what was in part a political rally. District staff handled several logistics for the event, organizing refreshments and helping with security.

“It clearly wasn’t to advocate for public education, our students or to share all that’s great about our schools,” she said. “It was to serve the Republican Party.”

![Image 2](https://www.the74million.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/trump-accounts-protesters-georgia.png)

Protesters line the route to Wheeler High School as. President Donald Trump’s motorcade passes by Wednesday in Marietta, Georgia. (Kevin Dietsch/Getty Images)

Several students also gathered in a Kroger parking lot a few miles away to protest the president coming to one of their high schools.

Some of Trump’s policies haven’t been popular at the school, where the majority of students are Black and Hispanic. In late January, Wheeler students were among the hundreds across Cobb County schools who walked out to protest immigration enforcement activities nationwide.

“I don’t love the fact that we are using a public school to promote a partisan political agenda,” said Edward Kim, a rising senior in the district.

Multiple candidates running for statewide office had a chance to make campaign pitches Wednesday.

State Sen. Brian Strickland, candidate for attorney general, said families are relocating to Georgia to leave blue states that supported former President Biden’s agenda.

“In November, we have a big decision to make,” he said. “We have to get this right.”

![Image 3](https://www.the74million.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/trump-2028-bus-wheeler-high-school-georgia.png)

As the crowds waited hours to get inside the school, a Trump 2028 bus drove back and forth in front of the campus. (Linda Jacobson/The 74)

Trump also told the attendees to vote for Rep. Mike Collins, who didn’t attend because of votes in Congress. Collins will face Democratic Sen. John Ossoff in November. Ossoff, he s

articles.title: In a Mississippi School District With Strict Gender Rules, Trans Students and Families Push to Be Heard
articles.summary: BILOXI, Miss. — When nine young adults tossed their mortarboards into the air at the Little Biloxi Theatre on June 20, it wasn’t their first time graduating from high school. After an emotional celebration that honored the transgender and LGBTQ+ students’ resilience, it became their first graduation without gender-based dress codes or stipulations on how […]
articles.full_body:
Title: In a Mississippi School District With Strict Gender Rules, Trans Students and Families Push to Be Heard

URL Source: https://www.the74million.org/article/in-a-mississippi-school-district-with-strict-gender-rules-trans-students-and-families-push-to-be-heard

Published Time: 2026-07-22T12:30:00-04:00

Markdown Content:
_Get stories like this delivered straight to your inbox._[**Sign up for The 74 Newsletter**](https://www.the74million.org/about/newsletters/?utm_source=website&utm_medium=article&utm_campaign=top&utm_id=newsletter)

BILOXI, Miss. — When nine young adults tossed their mortarboards into the air at the Little Biloxi Theatre on June 20, it wasn’t their first time graduating from high school. After an emotional celebration that honored the transgender and LGBTQ+ students’ resilience, it became their first graduation without gender-based dress codes or stipulations on how they present themselves.

Surrounded by friends, local allies and in some cases, family, each student walked across the stage, shook the hand of LGBTQ+ advocate Jensen Luke Matar, and received a “Pride! Graduation” certificate. With their names printed under the intersex-inclusive [Progress Pride flag](https://www.hrc.org/resources/lgbtq-pride-flags#:~:text=Progress%20Pride%20Flag,-The%20Progress%20Pride&text=Quasar%20added%20a%20white%2C%20pink,crisis%20in%201980s%20and%201990s.), each student was celebrated for graduating with “courage, authenticity and pride.”

“We love and appreciate our students, and we care to uplift and serve them always,” said Matar, director of the nonprofit organization Transgender Resources, Advocacy, Networking and Services Program, or TRANS Program, which was a sponsor of the event.

Students at the event told Mississippi Today that school policies like Harrison County’s restricting gender expression are harmful to their mental health and physical safety. The [Harrison County School District](https://files.smartsites.parentsquare.com/3771/hcsd_k-12_2025_2026__97.pdf) is unique among Gulf Coast counties for requiring students to dress according to their “biological sex that is stated in the student’s cumulative folder and permanent record.”

In contrast, student handbooks for school districts in [Hancock County](https://files-backend.assets.thrillshare.com/documents/asset/uploaded_file/5272/Hcsd/fafb6275-7ef1-400a-bc77-abdb2aab71e4/25-26_Secondary_Handbook_FINAL.pdf?disposition=inline), [Ocean Springs](https://www.ossdms.org/student-hub/handbooks/handbook-landing/~board/student-handbooks/post/dress-code), [Gulfport](https://resources.finalsite.net/images/v1749059849/gulfportschoolsorg/rcbsqeuanvvyxnvzm1pv/2025-2026SecondaryStudentHandbook.pdf), [Jackson County](https://resources.finalsite.net/images/v1778777302/jcsdms/trtcbhdhdd6wsgpsself/2025-2026StudentHandbookFinal.pdf), [Pascagoula-Gautier](https://files.smartsites.parentsquare.com/9982/2026-2027_student_handbook.pdf), [Pass Christian](https://4.files.edl.io/38cd/01/15/26/134709-744f8e35-47b4-4bee-b1e2-39ec5fab79ca.pdf), [Long Beach](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1n5xlEVzXd0oYV8hspPXw0myDP2bkKVLT/view) and [Biloxi](https://www.biloxischools.net/85180_3) do not include language that restricts students’ clothing choices to their sex assigned at birth.

![Image 1](https://www.the74million.org/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/lana-brown-trans-joy.jpg)

Lana Brown in the dress she wore to a Trans Joy event, in lieu of high school graduation. (Credit: Photo courtesy of Lana Brown)

For 2023 Harrison Central High School graduate Lana Brown, the June celebration was the first time she walked a graduation stage. Three years ago Harrison Central leaders told Brown, a transgender woman, that she could not wear a dress to her graduation ceremony. She chose not to attend, and filed a [lawsuit](https://dockets.justia.com/docket/mississippi/mssdce/1:2023cv00124/118690) against the school alleging that the dress code policy, enforced based on gender stereotypes, violated her rights. Although Brown lost the case, she knew at the time that it was a long shot, she told Mississippi Today. Both of her parents were fully supportive, and her dad told her that regardless of the outcome, it was a win to have the incident recorded.

This year, Brown was the first speaker at Pride Graduation, thrown by several local nonprofit programs. Wearing a formal black dress and in full glam, she addressed her fellow students, who had graduated high school since 2023.

“Three years ago I was, too, ripped from my graduation,” she said. “I was told that I could not walk across the stage unless I dressed like a boy.”

Out of her own experience, Brown said, she learned the importance of self-respect.

“You must respect yourself, and you have to not tolerate the disrespect of others,” she said to the graduates. “It won’t always be easy, but you have to stand up or you will be knocked down.”

Nearly two months ago, a Facebook post by D’Iberville High School [drew public attention](https://mississippitoday.org/2026/05/22/school-deadname-transgender/) to the school’s policies around gender nonconforming students. School officials, including then-Principal Cheryl Broadus and Harrison County schools Superintendent William Bentz have not responded to repeated requests for comment from Mississippi Today.

Brown said she doesn’t know what to say to Harrison County school officials, who in the years after her graduation have doubled down on the dress code. She said they know their policies hurt students.

“What do you say to one who already knows?” she said. “You’re turning a blind eye. You won’t listen to me.”

### **Advocates question district’s gender-based dress codes**

This was the first year of Pride Graduation, organized by advocates after Harrison County schools students faced gender-norm-based dress codes for senior portraits and the graduation ceremony. Due to the policies, several transgender and gender nonconforming students weren’t able to participate in graduation activities while affirming their identity.

A letter from the school district that a parent shared on social media, which Mississippi Today reviewed, outlined that male students had to wear a white button-down shirt and black pants, while female students could wear a dress or black slacks.

A group of parents and advocates are sharing their concerns about restrictive policies at school board meetings.

At the June 1 Harrison County school board meeting, the first after high school graduation, Noelle Nolan-Rider shared that in the 1960s, when Mississippi was undergoing forced integration, Black students were omitted from multiple yearbooks. Nolan-Rider was one of at least three people working with the TRANS Program who attended the meeting.

Nolan-Rider shared that the first Black woman to graduate from South Natchez High School was her 10th-grade English teacher.

“She is not in the yearbook,” Nolan-Rider said. “Her name is Patricia West, and it is discrimination when you do that. It

fact_packs.headline: In First School Visit, President Urges Atlanta Crowd to Support ‘Trump Accounts’
facts: Title: In First School Visit, President Urges Atlanta Crowd to Support ‘Trump Accounts’

_Get stories like this delivered straight to your inbox._[**Sign up for The 74 Newsletter**](https://www.the74million.org/about/newsletters/?utm_source=website&utm_medium=article&utm_campaign=top&utm_id=newsletter)

MARIETTA, Ga. — In his first public school visit since taking office last year, President Donald Trump stopped at an Atlanta-area high school Wednesday to promote his administration’s effort to kickstart savings accounts for the nation’s children.
For decades, he said, Washington politicians gave young people “nothing but debt,” he said.

“The government is finally giving money back to our children,” he told the crowd at Wheeler High School in Cobb County, inviting families who have opened accounts f to join him on stage with their children.

The event brought together several cabinet members who joined the president in urging families to open Trump Accounts. Before Trump took the stage in the school’s large gymnasium, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, Treasurer Brandon Beach, Education Secretary Linda McMahon and Kelly Loeffler, a former Georgia senator who now serves as head of the Small Business Administration, all described the child savings policy as transformational.

sources.title: In First School Visit, President Urges Atlanta Crowd to Support ‘Trump Accounts’
In First School Visit, President Urges Atlanta Crowd to Support ‘Trump Accounts’ - Yahoo
Trump highlights Republican candidates, urges Senate to pass SAVE Act during Cobb County visit - Georgia Recorder

fact_packs.headline: In a Mississippi School District With Strict Gender Rules, Trans Students and Families Push to Be Heard
facts: Title: In a Mississippi School District With Strict Gender Rules, Trans Students and Families Push to Be Heard

_Get stories like this delivered straight to your inbox._[**Sign up for The 74 Newsletter**](https://www.the74million.org/about/newsletters/?utm_source=website&utm_medium=article&utm_campaign=top&utm_id=newsletter)

BILOXI, Miss. — When nine young adults tossed their mortarboards into the air at the Little Biloxi Theatre on June 20, it wasn’t their first time graduating from high school. After an emotional celebration that honored the transgender and LGBTQ+ students’ resilience, it became their first graduation without gender-based dress codes or stipulations on how they present themselves.
Surrounded by friends, local allies and in some cases, family, each student walked across the stage, shook the hand of LGBTQ+ advocate Jensen Luke Matar, and received a “Pride!

Graduation” certificate. With their names printed under the intersex-inclusive [Progress Pride flag](https://www.hrc.org/resources/lgbtq-pride-flags#:~:text=Progress%20Pride%20Flag,-The%20Progress%20Pride&text=Quasar%20added%20a%20white%2C%20pink,crisis%20in%201980s%20and%201990s.), each student was celebrated for graduating with “courage, authenticity and pride.”

“We love and appreciate our students, and we care to uplift and serve them always,” said Matar, director of the nonprofit organization Transgender Resources, Advocacy, Networking and Services Program, or TRANS Program, which was a sponsor of the event.

sources.title: In a Mississippi School District With Strict Gender Rules, Trans Students and Families Push to Be Heard
In a Mississippi school district with strict gender rules, trans students and families push to be heard - Mississippi Today
ACLU challenges Mississippi high school over trans grad’s dress - Los Angeles Blade
